metadata.methodology: Blind A/B comparison: each prompt run with skill loaded vs vanilla Claude from the scaffold directory. Scored 1-5 on Content and Structure. Run with --disallowed-tools Edit,Write,NotebookEdit to prevent file mutations.
metadata.skills_consolidated: 8 → 2 skills (aztec-developer + review-contract). CMP target_skill fields below reference original skill names for historical context.

content.1: Fundamentally incorrect or missing. Would not compile/run. Key Aztec concepts wrong.
content.2: Partially correct but with significant errors. Some valid patterns mixed with wrong ones.
content.3: Mostly correct. Core approach right, minor issues (stale API, missing edge cases).
content.4: Correct and complete. Valid v4 code, covers main cases, follows best practices.
content.5: Excellent. Production-ready code, handles edge cases, includes non-obvious Aztec-specific patterns.

structure.1: Unorganized wall of text. No clear steps or structure.
structure.2: Some organization but hard to follow. Missing context or unclear ordering.
structure.3: Decent structure with clear sections. Usable but not copy-paste ready.
structure.4: Well-organized with clear flow. Code is directly usable with minor adaptation.
structure.5: Excellent structure. Copy-paste ready with inline explanations. Follows progressive disclosure.

test_cases.prompt: Write an Aztec contract that stores private voting records. Each user should be able to cast one vote privately, and the contract should tally results publicly.
grader_assertions: Uses a private state mechanism for votes (notes via #[note] macro, SingleUseClaim, or Owned/PrivateSet)
Uses PublicMutable for vote tallies
Private vote function enqueues a public tally update
Has a nullifier-based mechanism to prevent double-voting (via note nullifiers, SingleUseClaim, or explicit nullifier emission)
Constructor uses #[initializer] attribute
Correct function visibility attributes (#[external("private")], #[external("public")])
Uses self.msg_sender() for voter identity (not maybe_msg_sender().unwrap())
Private-to-public call uses enqueue_self with #[only_self] or similar access control on the public tally function
test_cases.key_signal: Nullifier-based double-vote prevention, private-to-public enqueue pattern, access control on tally

test_cases.prompt: Write a TypeScript deployment script that deploys the PodRacing contract to devnet. Use the aztec-starter project structure and utilities.
grader_assertions: Calls .simulate() before .send() for all transactions
Uses SponsoredFeePaymentMethod for fee payment
Imports contract artifact from local src/artifacts/ path
Deploys account before deploying contract
Uses AztecAddress.ZERO as from address for account deployment
Includes explicit timeout values in .send() wait options
Post-deployment verification (reads back state to confirm success)
test_cases.key_signal: simulate-before-send, SponsoredFPC, correct deploy ordering, AztecAddress.ZERO

test_cases.prompt: Write a complete E2E test for a two-player PodRacing game lifecycle. Include account setup, game creation, both players joining, playing 3 rounds, and finalizing. Use the aztec-starter project structure.
grader_assertions: Sets up 2 separate accounts with unique keys
Registers senders with wallet.registerSender()
Calls .simulate() before every .send()
Uses SponsoredFeePaymentMethod
Covers full contract lifecycle (multiple operations including state changes and reads)
Has appropriate Jest timeout (600000+ for beforeAll)
Checks TxStatus for transaction success
Uses .simulate() (not .send()) to test expected failures/reverts
test_cases.key_signal: Account setup ordering, registerSender, simulate-before-send, revert testing

test_cases.prompt: How does msg_sender work in Aztec? When is it None? How does it propagate through call chains? What are the privacy implications?
grader_assertions: Explains self.msg_sender() vs self.context.maybe_msg_sender()
States msg_sender is None at transaction entrypoints (account contracts)
States msg_sender is None for incognito enqueued public calls
Shows propagation chain: User → Account Contract → App Contract
Mentions enqueue_incognito() for privacy-preserving public calls
Warns that msg_sender in enqueued public calls is visible on-chain
Distinguishes private-to-private (composed in one proof) from private-to-public (enqueued)
test_cases.key_signal: maybe_msg_sender, incognito calls, on-chain visibility, propagation chain

test_cases.prompt: Create a complete TypeScript client that connects to an Aztec node, sets up a wallet, deploys a contract, and calls a method on it. Include proper error handling and fee payment.
grader_assertions: Uses createAztecNodeClient for node connection
Uses EmbeddedWallet.create() for wallet setup
Calls .simulate() before .send()
Uses SponsoredFeePaymentMethod
Uses correct import paths matching aztec-starter
Includes .send({ wait: { timeout } }) pattern (not .send().wait())
Uses getTimeouts() or explicit timeout values
test_cases.key_signal: simulate-before-send, correct imports, wait options in send

test_cases.prompt: Review the Token contract (src/main.nr) in the scaffold for security issues, correctness, and best practices.
grader_assertions: Produces structured output with severity levels (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
Does NOT falsely claim Noir u64/u128 arithmetic wraps on overflow (Noir integers PANIC, they do NOT wrap)
Does NOT falsely claim notes are missing randomness (the #[note] macro injects NoteHeader automatically)
Checks note ownership patterns (who can nullify notes)
Reviews access control on public functions
Checks function visibility attributes
Notes the private-to-public pattern in the shield function
Includes 'What's Done Well' section or equivalent positive findings
test_cases.key_signal: No false positives about Noir overflow or note randomness, structured severity output, note ownership review

test_cases.prompt: Set up a TXE test environment with 3 accounts and deploy a token contract. Include helper functions for setup reuse across multiple tests.
grader_assertions: Uses TestEnvironment::new() for initialization
Uses env.create_light_account() or env.create_contract_account()
Creates reusable setup helper function (unconstrained)
Defines TestAccounts and TestContracts structs
Uses env.deploy("Token").with_public_initializer() or similar
Tests are marked #[test] unconstrained
Does NOT use env.impersonate() (deprecated v3 API)
Uses env.call_private(account, ...) pattern (v4 API)
test_cases.key_signal: TestEnvironment setup, account types, v4 API (not impersonate)

test_cases.prompt: Explain partial notes in Aztec. How do they work at runtime? When should I use them? Show an example.
grader_assertions: Explains two-phase note construction (partial in public, completed in PXE)
Mentions PartialUintNote as the built-in type
Explains the runtime flow: private creates partial → public completes → PXE reconstructs
Use case: when the amount is only known at public execution time
Mentions that PXE reconstructs the full note from partial data + public completion
Shows code example with prepare_partial_note and complete patterns

test_cases.prompt: Walk me through creating a Schnorr account on Aztec, deploying it, saving the credentials, and later recovering the account from those credentials.
grader_assertions: Shows Fr.random() for secretKey, GrumpkinScalar.random() for signingKey, Fr.random() for salt
Uses wallet.createSchnorrAccount(secretKey, salt, signingKey)
Deploys with AztecAddress.ZERO as the from address
Calls .simulate() before .send() on deploy method
Shows saving credentials (SECRET, SIGNING_KEY, SALT) to .env
Shows recovery using the same credentials to recreate the account
Uses SponsoredFeePaymentMethod for deployment
test_cases.key_signal: Key generation, AztecAddress.ZERO, credential saving, recovery flow

test_cases.prompt: Explain the complete transaction lifecycle in Aztec. What happens from when a user initiates a transaction to when it's finalized? Cover private execution, kernel circuits, and public execution.
grader_assertions: Describes the three-phase model: Private → Kernel → Public
Explains that private execution happens client-side in the PXE
Explains kernel circuit aggregation of private function results
Explains enqueue semantics (private→public calls are not immediate)
Mentions that public execution is done by the sequencer
States that private reads of public state are from a historical snapshot
Mentions proof generation and submission to the network
test_cases.key_signal: Three-phase model, enqueue semantics, historical public state reads
